\begin{figure}[h!]
    \centering
    \includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{../input_files/plots/step_3_learning_curves_1_1776948522.png}
    \caption{Smoothed episode return versus environment steps for a standard PPO critic (Condition A) and a critic structured with a Lyapunov prior (Condition B). Curves show the mean ± one standard deviation over five random seeds, with individual seed trajectories overlaid. Both conditions exhibit nearly identical learning trajectories, improving initially before plateauing at a similar negative return value. This suggests that the structural prior in the value function did not lead to a discernible improvement in policy learning speed or final performance within the 100,000-step training horizon.}
    \label{fig:image0}
\end{figure}

\begin{figure}[h!]
    \centering
    \includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{../input_files/plots/value_function_heatmaps.png}
    \caption{Heatmaps of the value functions over the pendulum state space, comparing (a) the analytic Lyapunov function $\Phi(s)$, (b) the value function $V\_A(s)$ learned by a standard PPO critic, and (c) the value function $V\_B(s)$ from a structured critic decomposed as $V(s) = \Phi(s) + f\_\theta(s)$. Panel (d) shows the learned residual component $f\_\theta(s)$. The structured value function $V\_B(s)$ provides a substantially closer approximation to the analytic function $\Phi(s)$ than the directly learned $V\_A(s)$, achieving an 86\% reduction in mean squared error. The residual function $f\_\theta(s)$ is non-trivial, indicating that the network learned meaningful, policy-dependent corrections to the Lyapunov prior with an average magnitude of approximately 26\% relative to $\Phi(s)$.}
    \label{fig:image1}
\end{figure}
